query: definition of malignant pustule in dermatology

retrieved_docs.title: Koebner Phenomenon -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The Koebner phenomenon is not to be confused with pathergy, which is a term utilized to describe when minor trauma (including blunt trauma such as bumps and bruises) can induce skin lesions. [2] The lesions arising from pathergy tend to be non-specific papules or pustules that may develop into skin ulcers. Pathergy, commonly in conditions such as pyoderma gangrenosum Sweet syndrome and Bechet disease, is also used as a clinical diagnostic test. The site where pathergy positivity has been elicited often exhibits a monocytic infiltrate on histopathology. [2] In contrast, the lesions arising from the Koebner phenomenon reflect upon the patient's underlying skin disease, such as psoriasis or vitiligo.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypopigmented Macules -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Definitions of Macroscopic Terms
retrieved_docs.content: Macule - Flattened skin lesion less than 5mm in size, distinguished by discoloration compared to surrounding normal skin Patch - Macular skin lesion with more than 5mm in size Lichenification - Thickened and rough skin with prominent skin markings, often due to repeated rubbing

retrieved_docs.title: Papuloerythroderma of Ofuji -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The basis of the disease and initial diagnosis stems from an initial meta-analysis comprised of 170 patients via Torchia et al, who proposed a preliminary set of 5 major and 5 minor criteria that define Papuloerythroderma of Ofuji. These criteria help determine etiology and allow for categorization into 4 subtypes, which include primary, secondary, and a nonpapular form of Papuloerythroderma of Ofuji. [4] There is also a consideration for patients that appear clinically, like Papuloerythroderma of Ofuji, but have a diagnosis more consistent with lymphoma. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Lichenification -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Basic skin lesions divide into primary, secondary, and special types. The term lichenification is classed as a secondary skin lesion wherein the characteristic features of skin thickening, hyperpigmentation, and exaggerated skin lines are noted. Lichenification can be further divided into primary and secondary types wherein primary lichenification signifies lichen simplex chronicus, also known as neurodermatitis circumscripta. On the other hand, secondary lichenification occurs in atopic dermatitis, infective eczematous dermatoses, psoriasis, psoriasiform dermatosis, xerosis, pityriasis rubra pilaris, porokeratosis, vegetative growths, anxiety, and obsessive-compulsive disorders. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Lichenification -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The lesions start as hyperpigmentation on a flat skin surface that is followed by the appearance of multiple small papules, termed pseudopapules, giving the lesion a pebbly appearance. The next is thickening or induration of the deep layers of the skin that does not involve the skin creases and thereby imparts the rather characteristic exaggeration of skin markings. Lesions appear as ill-defined except for certain cases of secondary lichenification. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Laser Fitzpatrick Skin Type Recommendations -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: By nature of the criteria, Fitzpatrick's skin type classifications are subjective; however, use of the scale has proven valuable as a diagnostic and therapeutic evaluation tool for many situations. Specifically, these classifications are commonly employed to estimate dosages of particular ultraviolet B (UVB), psoralen with ultraviolet A (PUVA), and laser treatments, as well as to predict skin cancer risk and guide sun protection advice. [3] Though widely used throughout dermatology, plastic surgery, surgical oncology, and other specialties, the Fitzpatrick skin types are not without controversy. Current questions regarding the objective determination, accuracy of self-reporting, and effectiveness in ethnic skin are all major issues of concern with Fitzpatrick skin types. See Images. Fitzpatrick Skin Type Quiz, Questionnaire Pages 1 and 2.

retrieved_docs.title: Lichenification -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Diffuse types of lichenification have been described wherein involvement of the face, neck, chest, abdomen, flanks, arms, forearms, thighs and lower legs appear as ill-defined plaques ranging from hyperpigmented patches, numerous flat-topped papules, and indurated plaques may present symmetrically with extremity involvement. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Koplik Spots -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Koplik spots are the peculiar spots present on the buccal mucosa and are considered a diagnostic/pathognomic feature of measles/rubeola in the pre-eruptive stage. The term Koplik spot derives its name from Dr. Henry Koplik of New York, who first described them in 1896.

retrieved_docs.title: Teledermatology -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Telemedicine involves the use of telecommunication technologies to provide medical information and services. The word telemedicine is derived from the Greek root word "tele," meaning "distant." The application of the principles of telemedicine to dermatology is referred to as teledermatology. Classically teledermatology has been classified into real-time teledermatology and store-and-forward teledermatology. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Koebner Phenomenon -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The Koebner phenomenon (KP), first described in 1876 by Heinrich Koebner, is the appearance of new skin lesions on previously unaffected skin secondary to trauma. [1] This phenomenon is also termed the isomorphic (from Greek, “equal shape”) response, given that the new lesions appear clinically and histologically identical to the patient’s underlying cutaneous disease. [2] In other words, a patient with psoriasis who exhibits koebnerization (and is said to be “Koebner-positive”) develops new psoriasiform lesions along sites of skin injury, even if trivial. Koebner phenomenon can develop in any anatomic site, including in classic areas of psoriatic involvement and in regions that are usually spared, such as the face. The phenomenon shows dynamic behavior. Patients may be “Koebner-negative” at 1 point in life but may later become “Koebner-positive.” [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Urticaria Pigmentosa  -- Evaluation -- Major Criteria
retrieved_docs.content: Must have the typical skin lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: Urticaria Pigmentosa  -- Evaluation -- Major Criteria
retrieved_docs.content: Having multifocal, dense infiltrates of mast cells (aggregates ≥15 mast cells) in bone marrow or extracutaneous tissues.

retrieved_docs.title: Leser-Trélat Sign -- History and Physical -- Curth Postulates
retrieved_docs.content: Curth postulates were indicators created to help evaluate the temporal relationship between an underlying paraneoplastic syndrome and a specific dermatological condition. The diagnosis of a paraneoplastic syndrome was based on the following clinical criteria: [23] Both the neoplastic and paraneoplastic processes began concurrently. Both the neoplastic and paraneoplastic processes have a parallel disease course (ie, the paraneoplastic process resolves with treatment of the underlying malignancy, and the paraneoplastic process relapses if the malignancy returns). The paraneoplastic process is associated with specific types of malignancy. The skin lesions are not associated with any potential underlying genetic syndromes. The skin lesions are not common in the general population. A strong statistical association between the paraneoplastic process and truly having an underlying malignancy exists.

retrieved_docs.title: Pyoderma Gangrenosum -- Evaluation -- Major Criteria
retrieved_docs.content: Rapid progression of a painful, necrolytic cutaneous ulcer with an irregular, violaceous, and undermined border Other causes of cutaneous ulceration have been excluded

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Generalized Exanthematous Pustulosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The EuroSCAR criteria, published in 2001 by Sidoroff et al and often referred to as the "AGEP validation score," integrate clinical findings, laboratory data, and histopathology to stratify diagnostic likelihood. Scores of 0 or below exclude AGEP, scores of 1 to 4 remain indeterminate, scores of 5 to 7 indicate a probable diagnosis, and scores of 8 to 12 confirm the diagnosis. Outlined below are the EuroSCAR criteria used to establish diagnostic certainty of AGEP.

retrieved_docs.title: Koebner Phenomenon -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The Koebner phenomenon requires differentiation from the reverse Koebner phenomenon, which is the disappearance of skin lesions following trauma over the existing lesions. [3] When working on a differential diagnosis, it is also important to distinguish the Koebner phenomenon from the pseudo-Koebner phenomenon (also known as category II Koebner phenomenon as per the Boyd and Nelder classification), in which infectious agents (such as viruses) cause the spread of monomorphic lesions across previously unaffected skin (see Table. Classification of Koebner and Koebner-Related Responses). [4] The pseudo-Koebner phenomenon occurs with common infectious etiologies, such as molluscum contagiosum and verrucae [infective warts caused by human papillomavirus]. [5] See Image. Pseudo-Koebnerization.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2178
retrieved_docs.content: The medical concept “سرطان الجلد الخبيث” (malignant skin cancer) is mapped from the broader description “ورم ثانوي خبيث في مواضع محددة أخرى” (malignant secondary tumor in other specific locations), indicating that malignant skin cancer is understood as a form of malignant secondary tumor that can arise in particular anatomical sites. This relationship clarifies that the skin manifestation of a malignant secondary tumor is classified under the specific term “سرطان الجلد الخبيث,” linking the general category of secondary malignant neoplasms to its specific cutaneous presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2356
retrieved_docs.content: Other non‑epithelial cancer of skin is a broader classification that encompasses the condition described in Arabic as ورم خبيث في الجلد، آخر, indicating an additional malignant tumor of the skin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ and ربط البوق are medical concepts that frequently co‑occur, indicating that the presence of حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ is often associated with the finding or procedure ربط البوق. This consistent association suggests that clinicians should be aware of the link between these two entities, as recognizing one may prompt evaluation for the other, thereby informing diagnosis and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2666
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, a complication can be associated with the presence of multiple open wounds that themselves are not complicated, indicating that a broader clinical complication may arise even when each individual wound does not exhibit additional problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2675
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting an overlap in terminology where both terms describe the observable manifestations and patient‑reported experiences associated with poorly characterized medical disorders. This conceptual equivalence highlights the importance of recognizing that “symptoms” and “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” often refer to the same clinical features, facilitating clearer communication in diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting the overlap between patient‑reported experiences and observable clinical manifestations in poorly characterized disorders. In this framework, diseases and injuries are placed under the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many inadequately defined pathologies present with nonspecific clinical findings that are captured by this umbrella term. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 812
retrieved_docs.content: Burns, referred to in Arabic as حرق حراري, denote thermal injuries to the skin and underlying tissues; the term حرق حراري directly describes the heat‑induced nature of these lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2616
retrieved_docs.content: The term “kanker; metastase” denotes the broader concept of cancer with metastatic spread and includes as specific child entities the condition “سرطان الجلد النقيلي” (metastatic skin cancer) and the process “انتشار إلى العظم” (bone metastasis), indicating that cancer can manifest by spreading from the skin to other sites and by extending into bone tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2510
retrieved_docs.content: Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant, is linked to the Arabic term “النقيلة,” indicating that this word is used to refer to a lesion whose benign or malignant nature has not yet been determined.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2584
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is classified as a secondary malignancy and is characterized by the morphological pattern described by the Arabic term ال سرطان النقيلي, which denotes metastatic cancer. This relationship highlights that involvement of head and neck lymph nodes by metastatic carcinoma represents a spread of a primary tumor to a distant site, fitting the definition of secondary malignancy and reflecting the specific histopathologic features associated with metastatic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is a finding of disease and is categorized as cancer; other and unspecified primary, indicating that the presence of malignant cellular infiltrate reflects an underlying malignant neoplasm of unknown primary origin. Recognizing this finding is essential for guiding further diagnostic work‑up and management of patients with cancers where the primary site cannot be identified.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2719
retrieved_docs.content: The concept “kanker; metastase” (cancer metastasis) is linked by the “has child” relationship to the specific condition “سرطان الجلد النقيلي” (metastatic skin cancer), indicating that metastatic skin cancer is a child entity within the broader category of cancer metastasis. This relationship clarifies that “سرطان الجلد النقيلي” is a subtype of “kanker; metastase,” situating it within the overall framework of metastatic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, are identified as abnormal cells of disease and are classified under the category “cancer; other and unspecified primary,” indicating that these cells represent a form of cancer for which the primary site cannot be determined. This classification underscores the diagnostic uncertainty surrounding such tumor cells and highlights the need for further pathological evaluation to clarify their nature and guide appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1905
retrieved_docs.content: Kanker; metastase is a concept denoting metastatic cancer. Through the “has child” relationship, it includes the specific entities سرطان الجلد النقيلي (metastatic skin cancer) and انتشار إلى العظم (spread to bone). In addition, the “classified_as” relationship designates kanker; metastase as نقائل إلى العظم (bone metastasis). This hierarchy shows that bone metastasis serves as a primary classification for this metastatic cancer category, while also encompassing related manifestations such as metastatic skin cancer and the general process of cancer spreading to bone. Recognizing these connections aids clinicians in understanding the spectrum of metastatic disease and in planning appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.
